text: This video presentation is on a laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy, or WIPL procedure, which was carried out at the Division of Minimally Invasive Surgery, Department of Surgery, University of Florida, Jacksonville College of Medicine. The dunking technique, invagination of the resected pancreas into the jejunum. For our case presentation, we present a thirty-two-year-old female with a known diagnosis of solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreatic head confirmed by EUS with FNA. She presents with nine months of progressively worsening sharp and constant left upper quadrant abdominal pain, anorexia, and hyperglycemia. CT as shown here demonstrates a five by four by three point five centimeter lesion at the head of the pancreas.

The patient is positioned supine with both arms tucked. The surgical team uses a ligasure device to extensively kocherize the duodenum, reaching the ligament of Treitz from the right side. The right gastroepiploic vein is dissected, clipped, and divided, allowing access to the retropancreatic window where the plane is clearly identified.

The surgeon cross staples the antrum with two firings of a laparoscopic linear stapler. The common hepatic artery is identified, and the gastroduodenal artery is dissected using hook cautery, clipped, and divided. The bile duct is dissected away from the portal vein and divided with cold scissors. The neck of the pancreas is transected using hook cautery, noting the soft texture of the pancreas. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the jejunum is transected 15 centimeters distal to it using a laparoscopic linear stapler. The mesentery of the proximal jejunum is divided with a ligature device up to the ligament of Treitz, which is completely taken down. The jejunum is repositioned to the right infrahepatic area. The retropancreatic window is revisited, and the lesion is dissected away from the superior mesenteric vein using the ligasure device. The specimen is placed in a bag and retrieved through a suprapubic skin incision. Frozen section results are negative for all margins.

The surgeon creates an opening in the mesocolon using blunt dissection, transferring the stapled end of the jejunum into the supracolic compartment. A one-centimeter incision is made in the jejunum using a harmonic scalpel. The hepatico-jejunostomy is performed with a single layer of interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, using intracorporeal knots.

Due to the soft pancreas and unclear duct visualization, the surgeon performs a dunking technique. A posterior interrupted layer of Tuo silk sutures is placed between the pancreatic capsule and the seromuscular layer of the bowel. An enterotomy is created using the harmonic scalpel. A second posterior layer is sewn using a running suture technique with two-O proline. The first anterior inner layer is stitched with two-O proline, incorporating full thickness bites of the small bowel and the cut margin of the pancreas. Finally, the anterior outer layer is completed with two-O silk sutures between the seromuscular layer of the small bowel and the pancreatic capsule, resulting in a healthy-looking anastomosis.

The surgeon selects a segment of the jejunum approximately thirty centimeters distal to the hepaticojejunostomy for the gastrojejunostomy. Two stay sutures are placed between the seromuscular layer of the small bowel and the stomach wall. A small enterotomy and gastrotomy are created, and a common channel is constructed using a sixty millimeter laparoscopic linear stapler, which is then closed longitudinally with another firing of the stapler.

Final pathology came back solid pseudopapillary neoplasm, stage PT two and zero, zero lymph nodes, and a tumor size of five by four point five by three point five centimeters. At eight week follow up, the patient had improved abdominal pain and was recovering well with an excellent cosmetic result. In conclusion, laparoscopic pancreatic code duodenectomy is technically demanding. Laparoscopic dunking pancreatic cogegenostomy technique is useful in cases where the pancreatic duct is small in size, precluding safe duct to mucosa anastomosis. Thank you.
